summary: Drawing multiple large SKBitmaps with SKFilterQuality.High on SKGLView (macOS) causes significant frame rate drops when many images are visible simultaneously, due to per-frame CPU-to-GPU texture uploads and expensive bicubic resampling.

stepsToReproduce: Create a Xamarin.Mac app with SKGLView
Draw them in PaintSurface using DrawBitmap with SKFilterQuality.High paint
Zoom out so multiple images are visible simultaneously
Observe frame rate drop and sluggish pan/zoom
reproEvidence.environmentDetails: SkiaSharp 1.60.3 initially; later tested on 1.68.1. macOS, Xamarin.Mac, SKGLView (NSOpenGLView-based).

repoLinks.description: Reporter's playground repo with full source and repro
Skia forum thread with GPU texture caching recommendations
Skia forum thread explaining SKFilterQuality algorithms

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: DrawBitmap still creates a new SKImage.FromBitmap per call in current code, causing per-frame texture uploads. Performance pattern persists.

analysis.summary: Performance drops because DrawBitmap creates a raster-backed SKImage.FromBitmap on every PaintSurface call, causing repeated CPU-to-GPU texture uploads for each large bitmap each frame. SKFilterQuality.High (now deprecated, maps to bicubic/Mitchell resampling) further compounds this with expensive multi-sample filtering at downscale. The reporter partially addressed this by switching to pre-cached GPU-backed SKImages but still sees frame drops when all images are on screen simultaneously.
analysis.rationale: Classified as type/bug (medium severity) rather than type/question because the reporter expected GPU rendering to be smooth and the actual behavior is measurably degraded. The root cause is identifiable in SkiaSharp's DrawBitmap implementation (per-frame texture upload) and SKFilterQuality.High behavior. It qualifies as a performance bug even though workarounds exist.

keySignals.text: Since drawing is happening on the GPU (SKGLView) i expect smooth rendering and good performance
keySignals.interpretation: Reporter expected GPU acceleration to handle large bitmaps efficiently; instead gets CPU-bound texture uploads.

keySignals.text: The main improvement is to not draw bitmaps as they have to be copied each frame to the GPU. I create a intermediate image that is backed by a texture
keySignals.interpretation: Maintainer confirmed the root cause: SKBitmap forces CPU-to-GPU upload every frame.

keySignals.text: High - Adds bicubic filtering when upscaling (continues to use mipmaps for downscale). So, if you're using High, there is an abrupt algorithm change when the scale goes > 1, and the bicubic filter means many more samples for filtering.
keySignals.interpretation: SKFilterQuality.High (now deprecated) applies expensive bicubic resampling especially during zoom-in.

keySignals.text: once all images are visible on the view, performance takes a big hit and FPS go down. Once i drag the canvas so that some images are off-screen everything is smooth at ~60FPS
keySignals.interpretation: Even after switching to SKImage, visible-image count correlates with frame drops — GPU resource budget or overdraw may be the remaining cause.

codeInvestigation.finding: DrawBitmap(SKBitmap, ...) calls SKImage.FromBitmap(bitmap) on every invocation and immediately disposes the created SKImage — causing a full CPU-to-GPU texture upload per frame per bitmap. No caching occurs.

SKFilterQuality is marked [Obsolete] with replacement SKSamplingOptions. SKFilterQuality.High maps to SKCubicResampler.Mitchell (bicubic), which is the most expensive sampling option. Issue was filed before this API was deprecated.

SetResourceCacheLimits (plural) no longer exists; current API is SetResourceCacheLimit (singular, bytes only). The NullReferenceException the reporter got in 1.60.3 was from a missing GRContext guard; fixed in 1.68.1 per reporter's follow-up.
SKGLView on macOS is based on NSOpenGLView (OpenGL backend). GRContext is created and maintained by the view — passing GRContext to SKSurface.Create for GPU-backed images is the correct pattern.

workarounds: Pre-cache each SKBitmap as a GPU-backed SKImage once: use SKSurface.Create(grContext, true, new SKImageInfo(w, h)), draw the bitmap to it, call Flush(), then Snapshot(). Store the resulting SKImage and draw it each frame instead.

Use SKSamplingOptions instead of deprecated SKFilterQuality. For large downscales (zoom-out), SKSamplingOptions with Linear+Linear or even Nearest may give acceptable quality with much better performance.
Increase the GRContext resource cache via GRContext.SetResourceCacheLimit() to allow GPU to retain more texture budget across frames.
Implement frustum culling: only draw bitmaps whose destination rect intersects the current viewport to reduce GPU overdraw.
nextQuestions: Does the frame drop when all images are visible persist after pre-caching all bitmaps as GPU textures at startup? (Reporter noted partial improvement but still saw drops)
Is the remaining frame drop related to GPU overdraw (too many large overlapping textures) or GRContext cache eviction?
Does the issue reproduce on the current SkiaSharp version with Metal backend (SKGLView on macOS now uses OpenGL which is deprecated on macOS; Metal would be preferred)?

resolution.hypothesis: The primary cause is per-frame CPU-to-GPU texture upload from DrawBitmap + expensive bicubic filtering from SKFilterQuality.High. Pre-caching GPU-backed images addresses the upload cost; remaining frame drops when all images visible are likely due to GPU resource pressure or overdraw.

proposals.title: Pre-cache bitmaps as GPU-backed SKImages
proposals.description: Create GPU-backed SKImages once from each bitmap using the GRContext, then draw those cached images each frame.

proposals.title: Switch to SKSamplingOptions for quality control
proposals.description: Replace deprecated SKFilterQuality.High with SKSamplingOptions. Use SKSamplingOptions(SKFilterMode.Linear, SKMipmapMode.Linear) for zoom-out and SKSamplingOptions(SKCubicResampler.Mitchell) only when zoomed in.

proposals.title: Implement viewport culling
proposals.description: Skip drawing images whose destination rect does not intersect the current visible canvas rect to reduce GPU workload when many large images exist.

resolution.recommendedReason: Directly eliminates the per-frame CPU-to-GPU upload which is the primary bottleneck. Maintainer already confirmed and provided code for this approach.

actionability.reason: Workarounds were provided and partially helped but reporter still sees frame drops when all images are on screen. The remaining issue (GPU resource pressure vs overdraw vs cache eviction) needs further investigation or a repro on current SkiaSharp.

actions.description: Correct labels: set type/bug, area/SkiaSharp.Views, os/macOS, backend/OpenGL, tenet/performance
Post summary of root cause analysis and updated workarounds including SKSamplingOptions migration
actions.comment:
Thanks for the follow-up with the updated repro!

Based on the code investigation, there are two compounding causes:

1. **`DrawBitmap` re-uploads the texture every frame** — internally, `DrawBitmap` calls `SKImage.FromBitmap()` on every `PaintSurface` call, which triggers a full CPU→GPU texture transfer each frame for each bitmap. The fix is to pre-cache each bitmap as a GPU-backed `SKImage` once (as @mattleibow suggested above) and draw those cached images each frame.

2. **`SKFilterQuality.High` is expensive at upscale** — `High` maps to bicubic (Mitchell) resampling, which samples many more texels. Consider using `SKSamplingOptions` (which replaces the deprecated `SKFilterQuality`) and choosing the quality based on the current zoom level: bilinear for zoom-out, bicubic only for zoom-in.

For the remaining frame drop when all images are visible even with pre-cached `SKImage`s, consider:
- **Viewport culling**: skip `DrawImage` for images whose destination rect doesn't intersect the visible canvas rect
- **Increase GRContext budget**: `grContext.SetResourceCacheLimit(256 * 1024 * 1024)` (e.g. 256 MB)

Could you confirm whether the frame drop persists with the pre-cached GPU images on the latest SkiaSharp version?
